item.title: Birds of Paradise breeding active
item.description: THE Port Moresby Nature Park is driving a conservation effort, through its Birds of Paradise breeding programme, to protect the iconic species, while easing pressure on wild populations and strengthening long-term survival. Chief executive officer Johnpaul Houston said the Nature Park’s main objectives were on conservation, research and education.
item.content:encoded:
THE Port Moresby Nature Park is driving a conservation effort, through its Birds of Paradise breeding programme, to protect the iconic species, while easing pressure on wild populations and strengthening long-term survival.

Chief executive officer Johnpaul Houston said the Nature Park’s main objectives were on conservation, research and education.

The park’s breeding programme is a long-term, science driven effort to breed multiple species of birds of paradise in captivity, particularly the national bird, the Raggiana.

He said that by September next year, the park would host an international bird of paradise symposium to bring specialists together, and establish a special volunteer working group under the International Union for Conservation of Nature Centre for Species Survival (IUCN) for the research and conservation of these species.

The birds are kept within specialised facilities, such as the Plumes of Paradise precinct at the park, which comprises aviaries carefully designed to replicate natural conditions such as height for courtship displays, vegetation and the controlled separation of males and females to support breeding behaviour.

He explained that it was a capacity building project that the park uses to teach its keepers on what to look for, when to act, how to promote the birds to lay eggs and hatch them. Since then, the park has seen an increase in success of its breeding programme.

The park works with the Conservation and Environment Protection Authority (Cepa) – the Government entity that permits the park to keep these protected species of birds.

“Every year Cepa comes out to the park for inspections and the park provides a report four times a year,” he said.

Port Moresby Nature Park conservations manager Francis Gundu said the park relied on the rescue and rehabilitation of these birds to carry out its breeding programme.

“We only receive animals that are surrendered to the park by the public,” he added.

“We also work with the Royal Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (RSPCA) which bring to the park animals they confiscate.”

There are currently four birds of paradise species included in the breeding programme.

They are the Raggiana Bird of Paradise (Paradisaea Raggiana), Growling Riflebird (Ptiloris intercedens), Trumpet Manucode (Phonygammus keraudrenii) and Crinkle-collared Manucode (Manucodia chalybatus).

The Raggiana Bird of Paradise is part of an international breeding programme, while the other species are part of the institution’s breeding programme.

He explained that these species were successfully bred because the park had acquired a male and a female bird through the rescue and rehabilitation programme.

There have been eight successful outcomes from the programme since 2018, following the opening of the Plumes of Paradise precinct that was specifically built to study and support the breeding behaviour of the birds.

However, the park spent several years developing skills and conditions needed to successfully breed these species.

It was in 2021 that it had its first success with the breeding of a Raggiana Bird of Paradise chick.

Apart from the Raggiana, the park has also achieved the first captive breeding of the Growling Riflebird, highlighting its growing expertise in managing species that are notoriously difficult to breed.

Importantly, the breeding programme is part of an international conservation network, contributing to global efforts to preserve these species.

It also supports local conservation goals by reducing reliance on wild-caught birds for the use of traditional feathers, advancing national research on PNG’s native wildlife and educating the public through conservation campaigns and outreach.

There are three education campaigns carried out by the park during visits by schools.

They include the Lukautim Bilas Belong Yumi, City Kids Do Not Eat Bush Meat and Do Not Buy Wildlife as Pets.

Gundu said that the Lukautim Bilas Belong Yumi campaign was to teach the public alternative ways to source feathers for traditional attires.

The campaign teaches them that instead of constantly collecting new martials from the wild, especially birds of paradise feathers, people could care for and reuse existing traditional items, store them properly so they last longer.

Ultimately this would reduce the hunting for the birds of paradise.

The park also encourages passive learning through the placement of signage within the aviaries to educate visitors on the different species kept at the park.

He said that apart from their education programmes, the wildlife team at the park carried out Keeper Talks every weekend at the park.

It is where the public is educated on the three campaigns.

“We also have our animal ambassadors that are included during campaign talks.

item.title: Be social engineers to assist nation’s future, graduates told
item.description: A GROUP of graduates from the University of Papua New Guinea (UPNG) has been told to serve as “social engineers” and be the “heart of the nation” to ensure the country’s smooth progression into the future. Santos PNG country chairmen Leon Buskens, the guest speaker on the second day of the 71st UPNG graduation ceremony for the School of Humanities and Social Sciences in Port Moresby yesterday, sai
item.content:encoded:
A GROUP of graduates from the University of Papua New Guinea (UPNG) has been told to serve as “social engineers” and be the “heart of the nation” to ensure the country’s smooth progression into the future.

Santos PNG country chairmen Leon Buskens, the guest speaker on the second day of the 71st UPNG graduation ceremony for the School of Humanities and Social Sciences in Port Moresby yesterday, said that the progress of a nation was reflected in the quality of its humanity.

“For a nation to progress smoothly, it is its humanity that reflects it. And that’s you. Be the hearts of the country with humanities,” Buskens said.

“And that’s you, be the heart of the nation with humanities.

“You are the social engineers connecting the humanities of our people in PNG.”

Buskens told the graduates at the Sir John Guise stadium that the graduation was not merely the conclusion of years of academic rigor but also a “powerful beginning of futures full of possibilities”.

The School of Humanities and Social Sciences achieved a perfect 50/50 gender balance among its 294 (147 males and 147 female) graduates.

Buskens described it as a reflection of the evolving gender equality in the country.

He urged the graduates to use their training in the social sciences to bridge gaps between traditional values and modern development.

Buskens, who oversees social responsibility and community affairs for the energy company, reminded the graduates that they were now the primary architects of the country’s social infrastructure.

He encouraged them to maintain empathy and cultural understanding as they enter the workforce or pursue further studies.

The week-long series of graduations will end on Friday.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Sector set for digital shift
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea’s education sector is set for a major digital shift following the launch of five new applications aimed at improving transparency, efficiency and accountability across the system. Education secretary Dr Uke Kombra speaking during launch of the application in Port Moresby, described the initiative as a move away from the long-standing challenges of “lost files” and “delayed respons
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea’s education sector is set for a major digital shift following the launch of five new applications aimed at improving transparency, efficiency and accountability across the system.

Education secretary Dr Uke Kombra speaking during launch of the application in Port Moresby, described the initiative as a move away from the long-standing challenges of “lost files” and “delayed responses”.

“These applications represent a fundamental shift in our culture from ‘wait and see’ to ‘act and deliver,’” he said.

Kombra said the first app was the Electronic Resumption of Duty Summary Sheet, a system designed to address teacher payroll issues.

“The platform digitises salary processes, enabling real-time tracking of adjustments and automated verification of teacher data to ensure accurate and timely payments,” he said.

Kombra said complementing this was the second app called Teacher Pay Query System, a centralised help desk that allows teachers — particularly those in remote areas — to lodge and track payroll-related queries without travelling long distances.

“Each case is assigned a tracking number, with oversight provided through a monitoring dashboard.

“The third app is ‘Where is My School’ dashboard, a tool that would digitally track school infrastructure conditions and the distribution of learning materials across all 22 provinces,” he said.

Kombra said their fourth app was the Student Admission Application which would now monitor enrolment, attendance and student progression from elementary to Grade 12.

“With this data, we can accurately allocate resources, identify high dropout rates early, and ensure that no child in PNG is left behind,” he said.

“The fifth platform, the Government Free Education Policy Acquittal System, is aimed at strengthening financial accountability.

“It tracks the flow and use of school grants, providing real-time reporting and a digital audit trail to ensure funds are spent as intended.”

Kombra said the digital tools are designed not to replace staff, but to empower them to deliver services more effectively.

The secretary also reassured teachers and parents that the reforms would improve service delivery and ensure public funds were used responsibly to support students across the country.

Acting Education Minister Kinoka Feo, while acknowledging the importance of the application, said all these systems were part of one bigger goal, to build a better, stronger, and more reliable education system.

“They will help us manage teacher payroll more effectively, respond to issues faster, track students properly, and make better decisions using real-time data,” he said. “Most importantly, they will improve accountability, every Kina spent must be accounted for and every service must reach our schools, teachers and our students.

“This is part of the Government’s commitment to reform and digital transformation,” Feo said.

The acting Minister, however, made it clear to Education Department officials, school administration and teachers that technology alone would not address the issues and challenges in the country’s education system, but their management and administration would also need to complement the new technology.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Starlink will benefit education system: Department
item.description: EDUCATION Department Secretary Dr Uke Kombra has welcomed the outcome of the court decision regarding the admission of Starlink, noting that it would have significant impact on education throughout the country. “I’ve been one of those people who has been campaigning and asking for Starlink to come to PNG, especially for the benefit of our education system,” he said.
item.content:encoded:
EDUCATION Department Secretary Dr Uke Kombra has welcomed the outcome of the court decision regarding the admission of Starlink, noting that it would have significant impact on education throughout the country.

“I’ve been one of those people who has been campaigning and asking for Starlink to come to PNG, especially for the benefit of our education system,” he said.

He told The National during the launching of five digital applications yesterday in Port Moresby that Starlink will help change the way things are done in the education sector.

The applications are Electronic Resumption of Duty Summary Sheet (ERODSS), Teachers Salary Query System, where is My School App, Students Admission App and Government Free Education Policy (GFEP) Acquittal System.

“I’ve used a number of forums and conferences to advocate that we should allow Starlink or any other with similar system to Starlink to come.

“The bottom line of why I’ve been asking for this is to ensure that every school, every student, and every teacher has access to the internet so that we can be able to deliver education services,” he said.

He said education services in terms of teaching and learning, and in terms of teacher and school support.

“Therefore, we’ve been arguing for cheaper, reliable access to the internet, and this will come through Starlink and other providers that would come on board.

“We’re very happy and grateful with the court outcome because we believe that this would change the course of how we deliver education right through our system as well.

“We’re going to be using artificial intelligence (AI) in schools and improve how teachers teach and how students have access to resources,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: ‘PNG’s number one salesperson’: Marape defends approach to Ben Hunt on PNG Chiefs recruitment
item.description: Prime Minister James Marape has defended his approach to promoting Papua New Guinea’s rugby league ambitions, saying his engagement with players is respectful, transparent and within proper processes. “In promoting PNG whether in rugby league, foreign relations or business, I am PNG’s number one salesperson and promoter, so I have to respectfully do what’s good for my country,” Marape said.
item.content:encoded:
Prime Minister James Marape has defended his approach to promoting Papua New Guinea’s rugby league ambitions, saying his engagement with players is respectful, transparent and within proper processes.

“In promoting PNG whether in rugby league, foreign relations or business, I am PNG’s number one salesperson and promoter, so I have to respectfully do what’s good for my country,” Marape said.

Marape responded to Brisbane Broncos dummy-half Ben Hunt’s claims of aggressive recruitment tactics by the prime minister, describing the approach as “wheeling and dealing”.

Hunt told a Brisbane radio station that Marape attempted to “sweet-talk” players with offers of tax-free incentives and roles, also offering his club captain Adam Reynolds a coaching role with the PNG Chiefs.

However, Marape told The National that he spoke to players who came off contract and would consider joining the Chiefs setup, but stressed any discussions were subject to due process and overseen by team management.

“I talk to all players who I meet,” Marape said.

“If they come off contract and want to consider Chiefs, then this is what we offer, subject to the due processes which our team management have oversight on.

“I don’t overstep my country’s boundaries.”

Hunt made the comments on Wednesday after the PNG Chiefs announced Jarome Luai as player 001 as part of the expansion team’s recruitment drive.

Chiefs chief executive officer Lorna McPherson said Luai’s signing sent a strong message about the club’s ambition.

“Signing a player of Jarome’s experience and quality shows we are serious about building this club,” she said.

PNG Chiefs general manager (football) Michael Chammas said the New South Wales State of Origin representative and Samoan international was “a world-class player and a proven winner”.

Marape said the Government’s tax-free incentive to NRL players and officials involved with the Chiefs, was consistent with existing tax exemptions offered to businesses keen on investing in PNG.

“This tax-free status is similar to incentives we give to pioneering or targeted segments of our businesses or communities and is not an NRL initiative, so commentaries must not blame NRL,” he said.

“The vision behind the team extends beyond rugby league, positioning it as a platform for national unity and stronger regional ties.”

Marape said he respected the club management’s role in engaging in contractual discussions with players and officials.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Rising cricket star Frank gets call-up from PNG Barramundis
item.description: RISING cricket star Gaba Frank has earned first PNG Barramundis call-up after displaying sustaining performance in the Boroko Motors Isuzu T20 Smash season early this year. Frank is one of the exciting new inclusions in the national men’s senior cricket team earning his first national call-up after standout performances in the Boroko Motors-sponsored Isuzu Men’s T20 Smash 2026.
item.content:encoded:
RISING cricket star Gaba Frank has earned first PNG Barramundis call-up after displaying sustaining performance in the Boroko Motors Isuzu T20 Smash season early this year.

Frank is one of the exciting new inclusions in the national men’s senior cricket team earning his first national call-up after standout performances in the Boroko Motors-sponsored Isuzu Men’s T20 Smash 2026.

At just 18, Frank from Pari village in the National Capital District, has quickly emerged as one of PNG’s most promising young talents.

A left-handed batter, he has shown the ability to both anchor innings and accelerate when required, making him a valuable addition to the batting line-up.

He also brings balance to the side with his leg-spin bowling, while his sharp fielding continues to stand out as a key strength.

Frank’s development has been steady and impressive. He was the Under-19 men’s cricket team, Garamuts captain who led the national junior side to Samoa and later to the ICC Men’s U-19 World Cup EAP Qualifiers, Division-1, in Japan 2025.

He’s gained valuable leadership and international experience at a young age.

His development has also been recognised nationally, having been named Junior Male Athlete of the Year at the well-known SP Sports Awards in 2025.

When named in the squad on Monday, Frank said he had always looked forward to this moment ever since he first picked up a cricket bat and ball.

“This is a dream come true for me and I am really excited,” he said.

“I hope to play well and support the team so we can bring back the results we have trained and prepared for.”

Now stepping onto the senior international stage for the first time, Frank will be one to watch as he joins the Barramundis in their preparations for the upcoming ICC Men’s T20 World Cup EAP Qualifiers this month.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Vipers stay unbeaten
item.description: THE Moni Plus NCDC Port Moresby Vipers remain the only unbeaten side after a commanding 24-10 win over Gas Resource Central Dabaris in Round 3 of the 2026 Digicel ExxonMobil Cup at the Sir John Guise Stadium on Sunday. The Vipers crossed for four tries and controlled the second half to secure their third straight win, cementing their place at the top of the standings.
item.content:encoded:
THE Moni Plus NCDC Port Moresby Vipers remain the only unbeaten side after a commanding 24-10 win over Gas Resource Central Dabaris in Round 3 of the 2026 Digicel ExxonMobil Cup at the Sir John Guise Stadium on Sunday.

The Vipers crossed for four tries and controlled the second half to secure their third straight win, cementing their place at the top of the standings.

In one of the round’s closest battles, the PRK Mendi Muruks edged Asila Waghi Tumbe 22-20 at the National Sports Institute in Goroka, Eastern Highlands. Despite scoring fewer tries, the Muruks’ flawless goal-kicking proved decisive in sealing the narrow win.

At the same venue, the Lae Snax Tigers and Kandes Rugby League Club fought to a 20-20 draw, trading tries throughout with accurate kicking keeping the scores locked until fulltime.

In Rabaul, the East New Britain Agmark Gurias finished strongly to defeat the visiting Wamp Nga Mt Hagen Eagles 22-16. The Eagles led early, but the Gurias responded with four second-half tries and superior conversions to claim victory in a physical contest.

Another tight result saw the NPL Enga Mioks upset the Kroton Hela Wigmen 9-6 in a low-scoring clash at the Sir John Guise Stadium. A late field goal secured the Mioks’ win in the dying minutes.

Meanwhile, the Bintangor Goroka Lahanis held off a late surge from PRK Gulf Isou to record a 26-20 triumph, marking their second win of the season.

After three rounds, the Vipers lead the competition with three wins from three games. Close behind are the Gurias, Muruks, Lahanis, and Wigmen, each with two wins.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Barramundis announce team for T20 qualifier
item.description: THE Papua New Guinea national men’s cricket team, Barramundis, have confirmed their final squad for the ICC Men’s T20 World Cup East Asia-Pacific Qualifier, which will be held from May 8-18 in Japan. The team was announced yesterday at the Amini Park in Port Moresby. They will depart next Tuesday, following strong performances at the National Indigenous Cricket Championships in Mackay, Australia.
item.content:encoded:
THE Papua New Guinea national men’s cricket team, Barramundis, have confirmed their final squad for the ICC Men’s T20 World Cup East Asia-Pacific Qualifier, which will be held from May 8-18 in Japan.

The team was announced yesterday at the Amini Park in Port Moresby. They will depart next Tuesday, following strong performances at the National Indigenous Cricket Championships in Mackay, Australia.

The squad includes Assadollah Vala (captain), Sese Bau, Kabua Vagi Morea, Patrick Nou, Alei Nao, John Kariko, Hila Vare, Michael Charlie, Lega Siaka, Tony Ura, Boio Ray, Gaba Frank, Ryan Ani, and Aue Oru. Head coach Tim McCaskill will lead the team, assisted by John Ovia, physiotherapist Harrison Bourne, and team manager Hudson Leka.

Five changes have been made from the team that toured Oman in October last year. Tony Ura returns to the side, while Ryan Ani and Aue Oru have been handed their first call-ups. Gaba Frank, an 18-year-old left-handed batter and leg-spinner, is also included as one of the programme’s emerging prospects.

National selector Jamie Brazier said the squad reflects the growing competitiveness within the programme, noting that the changes highlight the depth of talent available.

Head coach McCaskill added that the team is well-prepared, with a strong mix of experience and youth, and is confident heading into the tournament. The ICC Men’s T20 World Cup EAP Qualifier serves as a key pathway event, with teams competing for progression to the ICC Men’s T20 World Cup.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: People must take responsibility for elections
item.description: THE countdown to General Election 2027 (GE27) began in earnest and without much fanfare yesterday which marks the start of exactly 365 days before writs are issued. Acting Electoral Commissioner Margaret Vagi has announced the tentative dates for GE27 as:
item.content:encoded:
THE countdown to General Election 2027 (GE27) began in earnest and without much fanfare yesterday which marks the start of exactly 365 days before writs are issued.

Acting Electoral Commissioner Margaret Vagi has announced the tentative dates for GE27 as:

Vagi has also warned eligible voters for GE27 to register during the Electoral Commission’s nationwide voter enrolment programme that starts next month.

She said her officers would visit all wards and local level government areas.

“You cannot vote in GE27 if your name is not on the electoral roll,” she said.

“The voter enrolment programme is to make sure eligible electors are registered and have their correct details on their ward electoral roll to exercise their democratic right to vote.”

New voters should be 18 years and above.

Those who are 17 years old this year, but will turn 18 before the issue of writs next year are also eligible.

Vagi said citizens must reside within their wards for six months or more and must enrol at that particular ward only.

“Voters who are on the electoral roll should check their names and details to confirm or update when the enrolment team visit their wards,” she said.

The rollout of the programme will be conducted from next month to October.

This statement cannot be simply dismissed as every citizen has a duty to elect the right leader into office if they want to see socio-economic changes after 2027.

Today, there are many who take to social media and every other platform to lambast government as a group or they take apart individual parties and leaders about what they consider as lack of development and progress in the country.

But you will find often that the same people who complain a lot do not make an effort to enroll for elections or to stand in line to vote when elections do come around.

This is irresponsible behavior that ultimately lands a bad leader in Parliament. A bad leader makes bad judgements and decisions which ultimately lead to lack of development.

Former Prime Minister Peter O’Neill put it rightly yesterday when he called upon everyone in the country to ensure they enrolled and that they vote.

It behooves everyone to heed the Electoral Commissioner and O’Neill’s call.

It is useless to decry a leader or a government one did not vote into office. People must have a basic understanding of the voting system and to make an effort to educate themselves if they are unaware.

Most people in the country tend to argue that in the grand scheme of things, their vote does not matter.

In areas where one political party holds a large majority of votes, it may seem that voting makes little difference, which can be deeply frustrating.

However, not voting is the one thing guaranteed to make no difference at all.

Even if voting will not affect the outcome of the result in an individual constituency, it can influence national votes for the parties.

Parties that do not win can still get their agendas taken seriously by the other parties if the amount of votes they get is high and get the Section 63 invitation to form government, as per the Organic Law on the Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates.

That is why every person who is not be happy with the current Government’s performance since 2019 to ensure they participate through the proper democratic process in GE27 to change the regime.

That is what the Prime Minister has been saying all along. He is challenging all to change him at the ballot box. It is a challenge everyone worth his or her salt should be taking up.

By the same token, anyone who is happy with his performance should be marching to the polls to ensure they get their votes in to keep Marape in office.

That is how it should work in a democracy.

From business houses to education institutions, all eligible voters must be allowed time next year to go to their respective polling booths to cast their ballots and exercise their right accorded in Section 50 of the Constitution.

These people must also play their part in educating others on the laws of land that govern elections in the country so as to allow for a better outcome of the election.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Resolve decades of Gobe Oil disputes
item.description: THE Gobe oil field straddles the Gulf and Southern Highlands and is Papua New Guinea’s first unitised project, combining multiple wellheads under a single management. The project has been embroiled in decades of legal battles over landownership and beneficiary rights, yet disputes remain unresolved despite numerous court orders.
item.content:encoded:
THE Gobe oil field straddles the Gulf and Southern Highlands and is Papua New Guinea’s first unitised project, combining multiple wellheads under a single management.

The project has been embroiled in decades of legal battles over landownership and beneficiary rights, yet disputes remain unresolved despite numerous court orders.

Curious about this, I visited the site, travelling from Moro near Lake Kutubu along the PNG liquefied natural gas pipeline road to Kikori in Gulf.

I observed that Gobe Main Petroleum Development Licence Four (PDL 4) and South East (SE) Gobe PDL Three (PDL 3) are geographically distinct, separated by kilometres and tribal boundaries.

Gobe Main PDL 4 sits on the foothills of Mt Kiki in Foi tribal territory, while SE Gobe PDL 3 lies east of the upper Kikori River, bordering Erave local level government and Upper Kikori, under Sau tribal lands.

The Kasere and Foi tribes are divided by Epo Creek, marking the provincial boundary.

The unitised model used in 1997 overlooked these local realities, relying instead on abstract frameworks like biocentrism and ecocentrism, detaching the project from the customary landowners.

This abstraction, along with political manoeuvring, has fuelled the ongoing disputes.

I recommend that the State appoint judges to revisit the land claims, with on-the-ground inspections and consultation of anthropological records.

Ground truthing should verify legitimate tribal claims, particularly those of the Foi people over Gobe Main PDL 4.

Courts must also investigate undue influences within Waigani, Mineral Resources Development Company, and the Department of Petroleum, following precedents like the KPHL saga, to resolve bureaucratic and corruption issues.

Cleaning up these disputes is essential for both the petroleum industry and the people of PNG.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Call for intervention over nepotism
item.description: A RECENT selection and recruitment exercise in a government department has sparked widespread complaints over unfair practices. It appears that staff appointments were dominated by one ethnic group, with several close relatives and associates of the secretary given key positions, including finance, internal audit, and corporate services.
item.content:encoded:
A RECENT selection and recruitment exercise in a government department has sparked widespread complaints over unfair practices.

It appears that staff appointments were dominated by one ethnic group, with several close relatives and associates of the secretary given key positions, including finance, internal audit, and corporate services.

The department previously hired a relative of the secretary at significant cost to develop a new structure.

That structure failed to reflect the department’s core functions, with cut-and-paste job descriptions leading to duplication and confusion across nearly all sections.

Notably, the same person who designed the flawed structure now holds a senior position and was part of the selection committee responsible for all appointments.

The selection committee also failed to apply subsection (5) under section 33 of the Public Service (Management) Act for positions not affected by the review, resulting in long-serving staff being displaced and rendered unattached.

Many new appointees were brought in through short-term contracts from outside the public service.

I call on the chief secretary to intervene and direct the Department of Personnel Management secretary to abolish the current structure and nullify all appointments and lay off externally recruited staff and those on short-term contracts.

These actions are urgently needed to restore fairness and proper function to the department.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: High cost of instability
item.description: THE ongoing Vote-of-No-Confidence (VoNC) debate, including the Supreme Court’s reserved ruling on standing in the case between Opposition Leader James Nomane and Prime Minister James Marape, highlights deeper constitutional and governance questions for Papua New Guinea. After nearly 50 years of independence, a pattern has emerged: disputes over VoNC procedures are frequently referred to the Suprem
item.content:encoded:
THE ongoing Vote-of-No-Confidence (VoNC) debate, including the Supreme Court’s reserved ruling on standing in the case between Opposition Leader James Nomane and Prime Minister James Marape, highlights deeper constitutional and governance questions for Papua New Guinea.

After nearly 50 years of independence, a pattern has emerged: disputes over VoNC procedures are frequently referred to the Supreme Court.

While this respects judicial authority, it raises concerns about Parliament’s ability to manage its own internal democratic processes with clarity and timeliness.

In principle, Parliament is responsible for determining whether motions meet constitutional and Standing Order requirements.

Repeated legal escalation suggests weaknesses in political trust, procedural clarity, and consensus-building, slowing democratic processes and creating governance uncertainty.

This is fundamentally a matter of institutional maturity.

A stable democracy requires Parliament to function effectively within its own rules, minimizing external arbitration on procedural disputes.

At the same time, national challenges – rising living costs, fiscal pressures, and governance scrutiny, such as Financial Action Task Force greylisting – demand stable leadership and consistent policy execution.

The VoNC is a constitutional right, not a threat.

Yet Papua New Guinea’s national interest requires stronger parliamentary maturity, clearer procedures, and greater political trust so that democratic processes are resolved internally, ensuring accountable, stable, and effective governance.

Reginald Renagi Strategic leadership and governance advisor-advocate

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pacific needs disaster displacement included in treaty
item.description: When disasters strike, so does displacement. Each year across the Pacific, cyclones, flooding, coastal erosion and storm surges prompt people to leave their homes.
item.content:encoded:
When disasters strike, so does displacement.

Each year across the Pacific, cyclones, flooding, coastal erosion and storm surges prompt people to leave their homes.

Often this movement is temporary, as people seek shelter in evacuation centres or on higher ground, returning home after the immediate danger has passed.

But sometimes it becomes prolonged or even permanent.

For many Pacific communities, displacement is not an exceptional outcome of disasters; it is a recurring and defining feature of how they are experienced.

Although Pacific countries are on the frontline of the climate crisis, they are also at the forefront of solutions.

The Pacific Regional Framework on Climate Mobility, endorsed by Pacific Leaders in 2023, is a world-leading document that seeks to ensure that the region is resilient and “future-ready” by anticipating, preparing for and responding to the adverse impacts of climate change and disasters, including through mobility.

The region has a new opportunity to show global leadership in this area.

Between now and early 2027, states are negotiating what is likely to become the first-ever global treaty on the Protection of Persons in the Event of Disasters (PPED treaty).

This presents a significant opening for Pacific states to ensure that displacement is reflected in the treaty text.

Despite how central displacement is to the lived reality of disasters, it remains only marginally reflected in the current draft treaty text.

The proposed treaty aims to fill a longstanding gap in international law by establishing a comprehensive legal framework to strengthen the protection of people affected by disasters, including through greater international cooperation and disaster risk reduction (DRR).

The negotiation process is now entering a critical stage. States were invited to submit written proposals for amendments to the draft text by the end of last year.

These proposals were discussed during a session of a dedicated United Nations (UN) Working Group early this month.

These discussions will inform a consolidated draft expected by September 2026, with a view to adoption at a diplomatic conference scheduled for early next year in the Philippines.

And yet, within this process, one issue stands out for its relative absence: disaster-related displacement.

This omission of displacement is striking given its scale and significance globally.

Disasters displace two persons every second.

In 2024 alone, an estimated 65.8 million internal displacements were recorded, with almost 70 per cent triggered by disasters rather than conflict.

Although the current definition of “disaster” (in draft article 3) mentions “mass displacement” as a possible defining characteristic of a disaster, it is otherwise missing from the treaty – and some proposals would remove even this reference.

The silence on displacement in the PPED treaty is not merely a technical oversight.

It risks sidelining one of the most common and significant consequences of disasters at precisely the moment when states are crafting the legal framework intended to address them.

If left unaddressed, the treaty may fall short of capturing how disasters are actually experienced by millions of people worldwide.

For the Pacific, this gap is particularly consequential.

Disaster-related displacement is a defining feature of the region’s security.

Pacific Island countries are among the most exposed in the world to cyclones, flooding, droughts and sea-level rise.

In some cases, entire communities face the prospect of displacement, which is why a number of Pacific states have developed national policies addressing internal displacement, as well as planned relocation guidelines and operating procedures.

Meanwhile, the Falepili Union between Tuvalu and Australia creates a special human mobility pathway which enables up to 280 Tuvaluans annually to migrate to Australia.

This leadership would be welcome in the PPED negotiations.

Pacific states have not yet made any formal proposals on the treaty text, and representation from the region in the Working Group discussions has also been limited.

item.title: Editha on track for art and design career
item.description: EDITHA Tubal has always loved arts and design since she was young, and yesterday, she graduated with a Bachelor of Arts degree in that very field – arts and design. “Growing up, I always loved art. I always try to put my hands to use. It wasn’t a second to question myself about taking up arts and design. I wanted to do that from the very beginning.”
item.content:encoded:
EDITHA Tubal has always loved arts and design since she was young, and yesterday, she graduated with a Bachelor of Arts degree in that very field – arts and design.

“Growing up, I always loved art. I always try to put my hands to use. It wasn’t a second to question myself about taking up arts and design. I wanted to do that from the very beginning.”

She scored a GPA of 4.33 to top her class at the University of PNG School of Humanities and Social Sciences. She described that achievement as “humbling”, after years of sleepless nights and struggling to achieve her goals.

Editha, 32, is the second last-born in a family of five. Her parents, Akuila Tabal and Fidelma Tubal, are from the villages of Taranga in Gazelle district, and Karavi in Kokopo, East New Britain.

Her mother Fidelma is a retired businesswoman. Her late father Akuila was a former public servant in East New Britain.

“I would like to dedicate (my degree) to both my parents, my mum and my late dad.”

She credits her mother as being the driving force behind her success after her dad passed away. She ensured that Editha was well and focused on her studies.

“My biggest supporter was my mum. She called me every day to check on how things were. That enabled me to come up with good grades.”

Her family including her mother, who flew in from East New Britain to support her on her big day, were at the Sir John Guise stadium yesterday to witness her graduation and to congratulate her.

Editha never doubted the path she was going to take regarding her career as she knew that art was what she wanted to pursue.

Growing up, I always loved art. I always try to put my hands to use. It wasn’t a second to question myself about taking up arts and design. I wanted to do that from the very beginning.”

From her early passion about art and design, Editha knew that her strength lay in her imaginative and creative traits. They helped her in “deciding things and putting them down on paper”.

Her biggest challenge at university were the nights when she would stay up late to finish her projects.

“The biggest challenge (for me) was probably the sleepless nights during the last few projects.”

Never giving up and always showing up in class helped her achieve her goal.

“I just made sure that I showed up every day. That’s half the work done. I showed up every day and I tried to do the best I could.”

Her support system are her friends, family and, of course, God.

Her word of advice to those still in school pursuing the same programme in the same field of art and design as her is to keep at it.

“It is very enjoyable. They can learn a lot from it when they are taking it.”

A career in art and design often focuses on the blend of creativity, practice, and the necessity of persistence.

Pablo Picasso, one of the most influential artists of the 20th century, when asked about art and design, wrote: “Inspiration exists, but it must find you working,”

And for Editha, earning the top award in her class means the world to her.

“It means a lot to me. I am very happy and humbled. And it makes me want to pursue a career in this field more.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Sex workers speak out about HIV in Papua New Guinea
item.description: Sex workers and their clients can often be seen in Papua New Guinea’s capital Port Moresby. Sylvia Pok, not her real name, is a mother who turned to sex work more than 10 years ago to make ends meet after her marriage broke down.
item.content:encoded:
Sex workers and their clients can often be seen in Papua New Guinea’s capital Port Moresby.

Sylvia Pok, not her real name, is a mother who turned to sex work more than 10 years ago to make ends meet after her marriage broke down.

“A lot of the time we are attacked and almost killed by these men,” Pok told the ABC .

She said it was hard and difficult work, but if she made K200-K300 from a client, the equivalent of US$60-US$95, she could pay her rent and feed herself.

“Even some widow mothers are now hanging around here and we look for money to fill our stomach,” she said.

But physical violence is not the only risk these workers face as PNG battles a surging HIV infection rate.

“There are men who are infected and hanging around these places too, and some come to just harm and kill,” she said.

In June last year, PNG declared an HIV crisis after 11,000 new infections were detected in 2024.

PNG health authorities estimated there was about 30 new HIV infections every day, including seven babies born with the virus.

Official government figures show that today there’s more than 120,000 persons living with HIV in the country, although the real number is thought to be much higher due to gaps in testing.

UNAIDS says sex workers, men who have sex with men and transgender persons are among the groups most vulnerable to HIV.

The latest data from the Health Department shows there’s at least 195,000 female sex workers and 358,000 male clients in the country.

Male sex workers, transgender sex workers and female clients are not separately counted.

Sylvia said clients often offered large sums of money for unprotected sex.

“If he refuses to be safe by using a condom then I will not have sex with him because my life is also important, and I am doing this to live,” she said.

Treena, not her real name, is a transgender woman who is a sex worker.

She’s been in and out of sex work for over 20 years.

“When my mother died in 2002 I just left school and started going into sex work just to survive,” Treena said.

She admits to having unprotected sex with men.

“If they give me a big amount of money, or buy me things, I just go for it, take the risk,”  she said.

The Health Ministry said that there was roughly 5,100 trans women across the country last year.

However, trans women have largely not spoken out about the challenge of combating HIV.

“This is not a good time that we’re going through,” Treena said.

Sylvia and Treena both said advocacy groups and healthcare providers visited sex workers on the street to test them for HIV. But the challenge facing authorities has been decades in the making.

During the 1990s, HIV cases rose steeply in PNG.

By the early 2000s, HIV became the leading cause of death at Port Moresby General Hospital.

Increased testing and availability of medicine in the 2010s saw HIV cases steady, but years of neglect and underfunding has led to one of the region’s worst health crises.

Sex workers, advocates and health workers all agree that the toughest challenge for persons living with HIV is still stigma and discrimination.

For the transgender community, visiting clinics can be daunting.

“Sometimes they’re not really friendly, but those that are sensitised they treat us well,” Treena said.
